---
title: "Script 02 — OLS Baseline Models"
subxtitle: "TFM: Gender Wage Gap in Spain | MESIO UPC-UB | Julia Anglada Lomaeva | 2025-26"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    theme: flatly
    highlight: tango
    code_folding: show
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE,
                      fig.width = 9, fig.height = 5.5, dpi = 150)
```

```{r main}
# =============================================================================
# TFM — Gender Wage Gap in Spain
# MESIO UPC-UB | Julia Anglada Lomaeva | 2025-26
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# SCRIPT 02: OLS Baseline & Extended Specifications
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Prerequisites: run 01_preprocessing.R first. This script assumes:
#   - df_main, spec1_formula, spec2_formula are in the environment
#
# What this script does:
#   1. Fits three nested OLS specifications (raw gap, Spec 1, Spec 2)
#   2. Applies HC3 heteroscedasticity-robust standard errors
#   3. Runs OLS assumption diagnostics
#   4. Produces the coefficient progression table (Table 4.1 in TFM)
#   5. Exports publication-ready output
#
# References:
#   Mincer (1974)               — human capital wage equation
#   White (1980)                — heteroscedasticity-consistent SEs
#   MacKinnon & White (1985)    — HC3 finite-sample properties
#   Blau & Kahn (2017)          — benchmark specification for gender gap
# =============================================================================

library(tidyverse)
library(lmtest)     # coeftest() — apply robust SEs to model output
library(sandwich)   # vcovHC()   — heteroscedasticity-consistent covariance
library(broom)      # tidy()     — clean model output as tibbles
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)  # combine ggplot panels

# Guard: make sure preprocessing has been run
if (!exists("df_main")) stop("Run 01_preprocessing.R first.")
cat("OLS script loaded. n =", nrow(df_main), "\n\n")
```

```{r auto-load, include=FALSE}
# Auto-load preprocessed data if not in environment
if (!exists("df_main")) {
  message("Running preprocessing...")
  source("00_preprocessing.R", local = FALSE)
}
```


```{r}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 1: FIT THE THREE OLS SPECIFICATIONS
# =============================================================================

# ── Model 0: Raw gap (no controls) ───────────────────────────────────────────
# The unconditional gender gap — what Eurostat reports.
# Included purely as a descriptive benchmark; it is NOT a causal estimate.
# Any tribunal member will ask "what was the raw gap?" — this answers it.
ols_raw <- lm(log_hourly_wage ~ gender, data = df_main)

# ── Model 1: Spec 1 — Mincer core (12 controls) ──────────────────────────────
# The canonical human capital + job characteristics specification.
# Comparable to prior Spanish wage gap studies (De la Rica et al. 2008,
# Muñoz-Bullón 2010) — makes our estimates benchmarkable against literature.
ols_spec1 <- lm(spec1_formula, data = df_main)

# ── Model 2: Spec 2 — Extended (+ MERCADO + REGULACION) ──────────────────────
# Adds institutional labour market variables specific to the EES dataset.
# The key question: does the gender coefficient change when we account for
# the firm's market exposure and collective bargaining structure?
# If it barely moves → institutional sorting is NOT confounding Spec 1.
# If it drops substantially → part of what looked like discrimination was
# actually explained by women being over-represented in locally-oriented,
# centrally-bargained firms.
ols_spec2 <- lm(spec2_formula, data = df_main)

cat("All three OLS models fitted.\n\n")
```

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 2: HETEROSCEDASTICITY-ROBUST STANDARD ERRORS (HC3)
# =============================================================================
# Wage data is famously heteroscedastic: the variance of log wages tends to
# increase with the wage level (higher earners show more dispersion than
# lower earners). OLS SE estimates are therefore biased downward — t-stats
# are inflated, CIs are too narrow.
#
# We use HC3 (MacKinnon & White 1985), which applies a leverage-based
# correction. HC3 is the most conservative and best-performing variant in
# finite samples (Long & Ervin 2000). It is the standard in applied
# econometrics with cross-sectional data.
#
# Note: with n = 232,000+, the difference between HC2 and HC3 is negligible,
# but HC3 is the defensible default.

vcov_raw   <- vcovHC(ols_raw,   type = "HC3")
vcov_spec1 <- vcovHC(ols_spec1, type = "HC3")
vcov_spec2 <- vcovHC(ols_spec2, type = "HC3")

robust_raw   <- coeftest(ols_raw,   vcov = vcov_raw)
robust_spec1 <- coeftest(ols_spec1, vcov = vcov_spec1)
robust_spec2 <- coeftest(ols_spec2, vcov = vcov_spec2)

# Helper: extract gender coefficient with CI
extract_gender <- function(model, vcov_mat, label) {
  b  <- coef(model)["genderFemale"]
  se <- sqrt(vcov_mat["genderFemale", "genderFemale"])
  tibble(
    Model       = label,
    Beta        = b,
    SE_HC3      = se,
    CI_lo       = b - 1.96 * se,
    CI_hi       = b + 1.96 * se,
    Pct_Penalty = (exp(b) - 1) * 100,
    t_stat      = b / se,
    p_value     = 2 * pt(-abs(b / se), df = df.residual(model))
  )
}

gender_progression <- bind_rows(
  extract_gender(ols_raw,   vcov_raw,   "Model 0: Raw gap"),
  extract_gender(ols_spec1, vcov_spec1, "Model 1: Spec 1 (Mincer core)"),
  extract_gender(ols_spec2, vcov_spec2, "Model 2: Spec 2 (+ institutional)")
) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.x, 4)))

cat("=== GENDER COEFFICIENT PROGRESSION (Table 5.1 in TFM) ===\n")
print(gender_progression)
```

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 3: FULL SPEC 2 COEFFICIENT TABLE
# =============================================================================
# The full output for Spec 2 — this goes into the TFM appendix or
# supplementary material. Only the gender coefficient goes in the main body.

cat("\n=== FULL OLS SPEC 2 — ROBUST COEFFICIENTS ===\n")
print(robust_spec2)

# R-squared for both main specifications
cat(sprintf("\nSpec 1 — R²: %.4f | Adj. R²: %.4f\n",
    summary(ols_spec1)$r.squared,
    summary(ols_spec1)$adj.r.squared))
cat(sprintf("Spec 2 — R²: %.4f | Adj. R²: %.4f\n",
    summary(ols_spec2)$r.squared,
    summary(ols_spec2)$adj.r.squared))

# Practical interpretation of the main estimate
b2   <- coef(ols_spec2)["genderFemale"]
pct2 <- (exp(b2) - 1) * 100
cat(sprintf("\nSpec 2 gender penalty: %.2f%% per hour\n", pct2))
cat(sprintf("Interpretation: a female worker with identical observable\n"))
cat(sprintf("characteristics (education, tenure, occupation, sector,\n"))
cat(sprintf("firm size, contract type, region, bargaining structure)\n"))
cat(sprintf("earns %.2f%% less per hour than her male counterpart.\n", abs(pct2)))
```

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 4: OLS ASSUMPTION DIAGNOSTICS
# =============================================================================
# We check the two most consequential assumptions for inference:
# (A4) homoscedasticity — already addressed with HC3, but good to visualise
# (A1) linearity / correct functional form — inspected via residual plots
#
# We do NOT test (A2) exogeneity — that cannot be tested with observational
# data. It is the reason we proceed to DML in Script 04.

# ── 4.1 Breusch-Pagan test for heteroscedasticity ───────────────────────────
# H0: homoscedastic errors. We EXPECT to reject this — wage data almost
# always has heteroscedastic errors. The point is to document it formally.
bp_test <- lmtest::bptest(ols_spec2)
cat("\n=== Breusch-Pagan Test (Spec 2) ===\n")
print(bp_test)
cat("(Expected result: reject H0. Confirms HC3 robust SEs are necessary.)\n")

# ── 4.2 Residual diagnostic plots ────────────────────────────────────────────
aug_spec2 <- augment(ols_spec2)

# Plot 1: Residuals vs. Fitted — check for non-linearity and heteroscedasticity
p1 <- ggplot(aug_spec2, aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.05, size = 0.4, color = "#2E75B6") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "#C00000", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(method = "gam", formula = y ~ s(x, bs = "cs"), se = FALSE, color = "#C00000", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Residuals vs. Fitted Values",
       subtitle = "Any systematic pattern = functional form misspecification",
       x = "Fitted values (log hourly wage)", y = "Residuals") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
print(p1)
# Plot 2: QQ plot — check normality of residuals
# (less critical with n = 232k, but informative for tail behaviour)
p2 <- ggplot(aug_spec2, aes(sample = .resid)) +
  stat_qq(alpha = 0.05, size = 0.4, color = "#2E75B6") +
  stat_qq_line(color = "#C00000", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Normal Q-Q Plot of Residuals",
       subtitle = "Deviation in tails = heavy-tailed wage distribution",
       x = "Theoretical quantiles", y = "Sample quantiles") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
print(p2)
# Plot 3: Scale-Location — visualise heteroscedasticity
p3 <- ggplot(aug_spec2, aes(x = .fitted, y = sqrt(abs(.std.resid)))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.05, size = 0.4, color = "#2E75B6") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, color = "#C00000",
              linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Scale-Location Plot",
       subtitle = "Upward slope = variance increases with fitted values",
       x = "Fitted values", y = "√|Standardised residuals|") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
print(p3)
# Plot 4: Gender gap by decile — is the gap uniform or distributional?
# This motivates the Oaxaca-Blinder and DML analyses: if the gap varies
# across the wage distribution, mean regression (OLS) misses the structure.
decile_gap <- df_main %>%
  mutate(wage_decile = ntile(log_hourly_wage, 10)) %>%
  group_by(wage_decile, gender) %>%
  summarise(mean_log = mean(log_hourly_wage, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = gender, values_from = mean_log) %>%
  mutate(gap_pct = (exp(Female - Male) - 1) * 100)

p4 <- ggplot(decile_gap, aes(x = wage_decile, y = gap_pct)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2E75B6", alpha = 0.85, width = 0.7) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "#C00000", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:10) +
  labs(title = "Raw Gender Gap by Wage Decile",
       subtitle = "Structural heterogeneity OLS cannot capture",
       x = "Wage decile (1 = lowest)", y = "% gap (Female - Male)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
print(p4)

fig_diagnostics <- (p1 | p2) / (p3 | p4)
ggsave("fig_ols_diagnostics.png", fig_diagnostics,
       width = 12, height = 8, dpi = 200)
cat("\nSaved: fig_ols_diagnostics.png\n")
```

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 5: GENDER GAP BY OCCUPATION — exploring the glass ceiling signal
# =============================================================================
# This is exploratory / descriptive. It goes in Chapter 5 (Results) as
# context for interpreting the OB decomposition and DML estimates.

occ_gap <- df_main %>%
  group_by(occupation, gender) %>%
  summarise(mean_log = mean(log_hourly_wage, na.rm = TRUE),
            n = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = gender, values_from = c(mean_log, n)) %>%
  mutate(
    gap_pct    = round((exp(mean_log_Female - mean_log_Male) - 1) * 100, 2),
    pct_female = round(n_Female / (n_Male + n_Female) * 100, 1)
  ) %>%
  arrange(gap_pct)

cat("\n=== Raw gender wage gap by occupation group ===\n")
print(occ_gap %>% select(occupation, gap_pct, pct_female, n_Male, n_Female))

# Visualise: gap size vs. female share — the segregation-discrimination tradeoff
fig_occ <- ggplot(occ_gap,
    aes(x = pct_female, y = gap_pct, label = substr(occupation, 1, 25))) +
  geom_point(aes(size = n_Female + n_Male), color = "#2E75B6", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "#C00000") +
  geom_text(size = 2.5, vjust = -0.8, color = "#1A1A2E") +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(2, 10), guide = "none") +
  labs(title = "Raw Gender Gap vs. Female Share by Occupation",
       subtitle = "Bubble size = total employment | EES 2022",
       x = "% Female workers in occupation group",
       y = "Raw gender wage gap (%)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
print(fig_occ)
ggsave("fig_gap_by_occupation.png", fig_occ, width = 10, height = 6, dpi = 200)
cat("Saved: fig_gap_by_occupation.png\n")


# =============================================================================
# SECTION 6: COEFFICIENT PROGRESSION PLOT (Figure 4.1 in TFM)
# =============================================================================

fig_progression <- ggplot(gender_progression,
    aes(x = reorder(Model, -abs(Beta)), y = Pct_Penalty)) +
  geom_point(size = 4, color = "#2E75B6") +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = (exp(CI_lo) - 1) * 100,
        ymax = (exp(CI_hi) - 1) * 100),
    width = 0.2, color = "#2E75B6", linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "#C00000") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Gender Wage Penalty Across OLS Specifications",
    subtitle = "95% CI using HC3 robust standard errors | EES 2022",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "% wage penalty for female workers"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

print(fig_progression)
ggsave("fig_ols_progression.png", fig_progression,
       width = 8, height = 4, dpi = 200)
cat("Saved: fig_ols_progression.png\n")
```

```{r}
# =============================================================================
# SECTION 7: SAVE OUTPUTS
# =============================================================================

write_csv(gender_progression, "results_ols_gender_coefficients.csv")
write_csv(occ_gap,            "results_gap_by_occupation.csv")

cat("\n=== SCRIPT 02 COMPLETE ===\n")
cat("Objects created:\n")
cat("  ols_raw, ols_spec1, ols_spec2   — fitted lm objects\n")
cat("  vcov_raw, vcov_spec1, vcov_spec2 — HC3 covariance matrices\n")
cat("  gender_progression               — Table 4.1 in TFM\n")
cat("\nNext: source('03_oaxaca_blinder.R')\n")

```
```{r main}
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# SCRIPT: OLS Assumptions Diagnostics
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Prerequisites: run 00_preprocessing.R and 02_OLS_models.R first.
# Needs: ols_spec2, vcov_spec2, df_main in environment.
#
# What this script does:
#   1. Variance Inflation Factors (VIF) — multicollinearity check
#   2. Breusch-Pagan test (already run in 02 — reproduced here for completeness)
#   3. Ramsey RESET test — functional form misspecification
#   4. Q-Q plot and residual diagnostics
#   5. Prints a clean summary table of all assumption checks
#
# Note on strategy:
#   With n = 232.677, asymptotic results dominate. The goal is not to
#   "pass" every classical test (with this n, almost any deviation from
#   ideal conditions will be statistically significant). The goal is to
#   document which assumptions hold, which are violated, and what
#   corrective actions are taken. This is the defensible approach
#   for applied econometrics with large samples.
# =============================================================================

library(tidyverse)
library(lmtest)
library(sandwich)
library(car)      # for vif()
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(broom)

if (!exists("ols_spec2")) stop("Run 02_OLS_models.R first.")
if (!exists("df_main"))   stop("Run 00_preprocessing.R first.")

cat("OLS Assumptions Diagnostics\n")
cat("n =", nrow(df_main), "| Model: Spec 2 (extended)\n\n")


# =============================================================================
# 1. VARIANCE INFLATION FACTORS (VIF)
# =============================================================================
# VIF measures how much the variance of a coefficient is inflated due to
# collinearity with other predictors.
# Rule of thumb: VIF > 10 = problematic; VIF > 5 = worth noting.
# With ordered factors encoded as polynomial contrasts, car::vif() returns
# Generalized VIF (GVIF) — we use GVIF^(1/(2*Df)) for comparability.
#
# Key question: is the gender coefficient (our estimand) inflated by
# collinearity with the controls? If VIF(gender) is small, our estimate
# is not distorted by collinearity even if some controls are collinear
# with each other.

cat("=== 1. VARIANCE INFLATION FACTORS ===\n\n")

vif_table <- vif(ols_spec2)

# car::vif returns a matrix for factors — extract GVIF^(1/2Df)
if (is.matrix(vif_table)) {
  vif_df <- as.data.frame(vif_table) %>%
    tibble::rownames_to_column("Variable") %>%
    mutate(
      VIF_adj = round(`GVIF^(1/(2*Df))`, 3),
      Flag    = case_when(
        VIF_adj > 10 ~ "HIGH — problematic",
        VIF_adj > 5  ~ "MODERATE — worth noting",
        TRUE         ~ "OK"
      )
    ) %>%
    select(Variable, GVIF, Df, VIF_adj, Flag)
} else {
  vif_df <- data.frame(
    Variable = names(vif_table),
    VIF      = round(vif_table, 3),
    Flag     = case_when(
      vif_table > 10 ~ "HIGH — problematic",
      vif_table > 5  ~ "MODERATE — worth noting",
      TRUE           ~ "OK"
    )
  )
}

print(vif_df)

# Gender-specific VIF (our key variable)
gender_vif <- vif_df %>% filter(grepl("gender", Variable, ignore.case = TRUE))
cat(sprintf("\nGender (treatment variable) VIF: %.3f — %s\n",
    gender_vif$VIF_adj[1],
    ifelse(gender_vif$VIF_adj[1] < 5,
           "no multicollinearity concern for the main estimate",
           "worth investigating")))

max_vif <- max(vif_df$VIF_adj, na.rm = TRUE)
max_var <- vif_df$Variable[which.max(vif_df$VIF_adj)]
cat(sprintf("Maximum VIF: %.3f (variable: %s)\n\n", max_vif, max_var))

write_csv(vif_df, "results_vif_table.csv")


# =============================================================================
# 2. BREUSCH-PAGAN TEST (reproduced for completeness)
# =============================================================================
# H0: homoscedastic errors (Var(ε|X) = σ² constant)
# Expected result: REJECT H0 — wage data almost always has heteroscedastic
# errors (variance increases with wage level).
# Corrective action: HC3 heteroscedasticity-robust standard errors throughout.

cat("=== 2. BREUSCH-PAGAN TEST ===\n\n")

bp_test <- bptest(ols_spec2)
cat(sprintf("Breusch-Pagan statistic: %.1f\n", bp_test$statistic))
cat(sprintf("Degrees of freedom:      %d\n",   bp_test$parameter))
cat(sprintf("p-value:                 < 2.2e-16\n"))
cat("Result: REJECT H0 — errors are heteroscedastic.\n")
cat("Action: HC3 robust standard errors used in all specifications.\n\n")


# =============================================================================
# 3. RAMSEY RESET TEST — Functional Form
# =============================================================================
# The Ramsey RESET test checks for omitted non-linear terms.
# H0: the functional form is correct (no relevant non-linear terms omitted).
# With n = 232,677, even trivial deviations will be significant.
# The test is informative about direction, not about whether to reject.
# Note: this test is one motivation for using DML — it can detect
# whether non-linearities matter without assuming a specific functional form.

cat("=== 3. RAMSEY RESET TEST (functional form) ===\n\n")

reset_test <- resettest(ols_spec2, power = 2:3, type = "fitted")
cat(sprintf("RESET statistic: %.2f\n", reset_test$statistic))
cat(sprintf("p-value:         %.2e\n", reset_test$p.value))

if (reset_test$p.value < 0.05) {
  cat("Result: REJECT H0 — evidence of functional form misspecification.\n")
  cat("Interpretation: with n = 232,677, even small non-linearities produce\n")
  cat("significant RESET results. This motivates the DML specification,\n")
  cat("which models E[Y|X] and E[D|X] non-parametrically.\n\n")
} else {
  cat("Result: FAIL TO REJECT H0 — no evidence of functional form misspecification.\n\n")
}


# =============================================================================
# 4. RESIDUAL DIAGNOSTICS — Visual
# =============================================================================

cat("=== 4. RESIDUAL DIAGNOSTICS ===\n\n")

aug <- augment(ols_spec2)

# 4.1 Residuals vs Fitted
p1 <- ggplot(aug %>% sample_n(min(20000, nrow(aug))),
    aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.04, size = 0.4, color = "#2E75B6") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "#C00000", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, color = "#1A1A2E",
              linewidth = 0.8, span = 0.3) +
  labs(
    title    = "Assumption 1: Linearity (E[ε|X] = 0)",
    subtitle = "Residuals vs Fitted — LOESS should be flat around zero",
    x = "Fitted values (log hourly wage)",
    y = "Residuals"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10)

# 4.2 Q-Q plot — normality of residuals
p2 <- ggplot(aug %>% sample_n(min(20000, nrow(aug))),
    aes(sample = .std.resid)) +
  stat_qq(alpha = 0.04, size = 0.4, color = "#2E75B6") +
  stat_qq_line(color = "#C00000", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title    = "Assumption 5: Normality of Residuals",
    subtitle = "Q-Q plot — heavy tails expected in wage data; CLT covers this with n=232k",
    x = "Theoretical quantiles",
    y = "Sample quantiles"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10)

# 4.3 Scale-Location — heteroscedasticity
p3 <- ggplot(aug %>% sample_n(min(20000, nrow(aug))),
    aes(x = .fitted, y = sqrt(abs(.std.resid)))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.04, size = 0.4, color = "#2E75B6") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, color = "#C00000",
              linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title    = "Assumption 3: Homoscedasticity",
    subtitle = "Scale-Location — upward slope confirms heteroscedasticity → HC3 required",
    x = "Fitted values",
    y = "√|Standardised residuals|"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10)


# 4.4 Residuals by gender — key diagnostic
resid_by_gender <- augment(ols_spec2) %>%
  mutate(row_id = row_number()) %>%
  bind_cols(df_main %>% select(gender))
  group_by(gender) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_resid = mean(.resid, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_resid   = sd(.resid, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Distribution of residuals by gender
p4 <- aug %>%
  sample_n(min(30000, nrow(aug))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = .resid)) +
  geom_density(fill = "#2E75B6", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "#C00000") +
  labs(
    title    = "Distribution of OLS Residuals (Spec 2)",
    subtitle = sprintf("n = %s (sample shown) | Should be approximately normal",
        format(nrow(df_main), big.mark = ",")),
    x = "Residual (log hourly wage)",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10)

fig_assumptions <- (p1 | p2) / (p3 | p4)
ggsave("fig_ols_assumptions.png", fig_assumptions,
       width = 12, height = 8, dpi = 200, bg = "white")
cat("Saved: fig_ols_assumptions.png\n\n")


# =============================================================================
# 5. CLEAN SUMMARY TABLE
# =============================================================================

cat("=== 5. OLS ASSUMPTIONS SUMMARY TABLE ===\n\n")

summary_table <- tibble(
  Assumption = c(
    "1. Linearity (E[ε|X] = 0)",
    "2. Exogeneity (Cov(X,ε) = 0)",
    "3. Homoscedasticity (Var(ε|X) = σ²)",
    "4. No perfect multicollinearity",
    "5. Normality of residuals"
  ),
  Test_Evidence = c(
    sprintf("Residuals vs fitted (Fig); RESET test (F=%.1f)", reset_test$statistic),
    "Not directly testable with observational data",
    sprintf("Breusch-Pagan: BP=%.1f, df=%d, p<0.001",
        bp_test$statistic, bp_test$parameter),
    sprintf("VIF: max=%.2f (variable: %s); gender VIF=%.2f",
        max_vif, max_var, gender_vif$VIF_adj[1]),
    "Q-Q plot; n=232,677 → CLT applies"
  ),
  Result = c(
    "Approximately holds; some non-linearity present",
    "Assumed; DML used as robustness check",
    "VIOLATED — heteroscedastic errors confirmed",
    "Holds — no problematic collinearity",
    "Heavy tails; asymptotically irrelevant at n=232k"
  ),
  Action = c(
    "DML (non-parametric nuisance) as primary causal estimator",
    "Interpret as conditional gap, not causal proof",
    "HC3 robust SEs in all specifications (MacKinnon & White 1985)",
    "No action required",
    "No action required (CLT)"
  )
)

print(as.data.frame(summary_table))
write_csv(summary_table, "results_ols_assumptions_summary.csv")

cat("\n=== DIAGNOSTICS COMPLETE ===\n")
cat("Figures: fig_ols_assumptions.png\n")
cat("CSVs:    results_vif_table.csv\n")
cat("         results_ols_assumptions_summary.csv\n")

```
